topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the graduate seminar room seemed to buzz with a frantic energy, mirroring the state of my own mind. Dr. Albright’s announcement, delivered with a practiced, gentle cadence, hung in the air like a sudden drop in temperature. He was leaving. My thesis advisor, the architect of my entire research trajectory, was departing. I gripped the edge of my desk until my knuckles turned white, the cheap laminate digging into my palms. Every meticulously organized folder on my laptop screen suddenly seemed to vibrate with impending chaos. I stared at the stack of annotated bibliographies, the neat handwriting dissolving into meaningless squiggles. A tightness began to constrict in my chest, making each breath shallow. I wanted to stand up, pace the cramped space, maybe smash something—anything—to release the pressure building behind my eyes.

[story 2]

"So, that's it then," Dr. Albright said, his smile strained, as he packed his worn leather satchel. The sound of his footsteps receding down the hallway was unnervingly loud. I didn't move. I just sat, the chair suddenly feeling too small, too flimsy. My gaze drifted to the whiteboard, where his carefully mapped-out timeline for my project was still sketched in dry-erase marker. It looked like a roadmap leading into a sheer, unnavigable cliff face. A cold sweat prickled my hairline. I picked up my pen, but the ink seemed to swim, refusing to form a coherent line. My throat felt thick, like I’d swallowed sandpaper. I began tapping my foot rapidly against the floor, a nervous, uneven rhythm that betrayed the frantic churning inside.

[story 3]

I reread the final email, the subject line stark and impersonal. The words felt distant, like they were being spoken by someone else. My hands trembled as I set the printed notification face down on the desk. The silence in my small apartment was oppressive, amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to see the indifferent sprawl of the city below. The sheer volume of uncompleted tasks—the data analysis pending, the literature review unfinished, the entire structure of my argument suddenly unsupported—pressed down on my shoulders. I pressed the heels of my hands into my eyes, trying to physically squeeze the swirling uncertainty out of my skull. A deep, shaky sigh escaped, ragged and uncontrolled.
